LITERATURE REVIEW FOR ATXi (GLPG1690)/PERKi (AMG PERK 44) OPTIMIZATION PLAN

Matilda Salko 07/2021

Elina – tästä meitä kiinnostavasta GLPG1690:sta ei löytynyt juurikaan in vitro -tutkimuksia PubMedistä. PERKi-inhibiittori AMG PERK 44:n suhteen vielä heikommat löydökset (haasteena on tietysti saattanut olla myös etsijän tiedonhakutaidot…). Tässä alussa on vähän muistiinpanoja taustasta ja LPA-pathwaysta ylipäätään, lähinnä mulle itselleni opetukseksi. Alempana ne edes suhteellisen relevantilta vaikuttavat artikkelit, joita löysin.

Information from Juan R Cubillos-Ruiz: “These are the conditions that we would suggest testing in your system to determine if they are able to modulate (hopefully improve) response to IO + PARPi:

Autotaxin inhibitor GLPG1690 used at final concentrations ranging from 0.2 - 1 uM

PERK inhibitor AMG PERK 44 used at final concentrations ranging from 0.2 - 1 uM

Combination of 1 and 2. above “

LPA (lysophosphatidic acid) in tumors

PMID: 33526777 (review 2021):

LPA is a water-soluble phospholipid, which, by current knowledge, acts through 6 g protein-coupled receptors: LPAR1-LPAR6.

In addition GPR87 (g protein-coupled receptor 87) and (orphan) P2Y10

TRPV1 (transient receptor potential vanilloid 1) responds to LPA stimuli, in addition to other protein-mediated stimuli.

Produced during cell membrane synthesis and acts as a robust extracellular signaling molecule present in tissues and blood plasma.

LPA-mediated signaling is responsible for proliferation, migration, and cytoskeletal reorganization, to name a few.

LPA is present in all eukaryotic tissues and fluids, both in physiological and pathological conditions

LPA can be present both intra- and extracellularly

One major pathway involved in LPA extracellular synthesis, where lysophospholipids (LPs, which are produced from membrane phospholipid precursors), are converted into LPA by autotaxin (ATX; ENPP2).

In (solid) cancers, increased LPA signaling is related to numerous roles:

LPAR1 and LPAR4 - mostly associated with cell motility and invasion

LPAR4 is strongly expressed in ovaries under physiological conditions

LPAR2 - cell survival and apoptosis inhibition

LPAR3 and LPAR6 - promotion of tumor proliferation, migration, invasiveness, aggressiveness, and progression of different cancers

Also, LPAR3 has been related to the regulation of dendritic cell migration, and possibly having an antitumor immunity role

LPAR5 - involved in immune response inhibition and cancer progression

ATX and LPA are released by both tumor cells and other cells in the TME, increasing invasion, suppressing antitumor response and contributing to therapy resistance.

LPA-LPAR signaling has also been demonstrated to promote EMT

LPA has also been shown to induce the production of proinflammatory cytokines, such as IL6 and IL8 - impacting the progression and metastasis. An inflammatory environment creates a positive loop where LPA-LPAR signaling is enhanced

Increased LPA signaling has been linked to carcinogenesis and tumor progression as well as reprogramming of metastatic microenvironments and development of therapy resistance

One study taken into account in this review reported that chemotherapy inhibits expression of LPAR1-3

In addition, there is a positive loop in which the inflammatory milieu induces more LPA-LPAR signaling and chronic inflammation can lead to malignant transformation.

LPA receptors and signaling intermediates. LPA signals through six different LPA transmembrane G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs and LPAR1–6) and other GPCRs (such as P2Y10 and GRP87). These receptors activate different intracellular signaling mediators to elicit different/several cellular responses (such as cytoskeleton reorganization, migration, proliferation, survival, and cell–cell communication). Finally, LPA was also recently described as a direct PPARγ agonist and in this sense intracellular LPA could also induce signaling through this receptor. Scheme created using Biorender.com

In ovarian cancer:

“LPA has a tremendously described influence on ovarian cancer. It acts on the EMT, glucose metabolism, cell migration, among other roles; and also functions as a biomarker.” (ref PMID: 33526777).

In ovarian cancer, LPA affects the EMT, glucose metabolism, cell migration, and other factors

LPA-promoted glycolytic shift could lead to transformation of normal fibroblasts to CAFs

LPA Induces Metabolic Reprogramming in Ovarian Cancer via a Pseudohypoxic Response (2018, PMID: 29386184)

Study used patient-derived ovarian cancer cells (isolated from ascites samples), and HGSOC cell lines (ex. Kuramochi, OVCAR8, SKOV3-ip among others) to demonstrate the effect of LPA signaling on metabolism of OC cells

LPA induced an increase in aerobic glycolysis and glycolytic capacity in all tested ovarian cancer cells

Ovarian Cancer Cell-derived Lysophosphatidic Acid Induces Glycolytic Shift and Cancer-associated Fibroblast-Phenotype in Normal and Peritumoral Fibroblasts (2019, PMID: 30503552)

Experiment done on immortalized normal ovarian fibroblasts and ovarian CAFs (CAF147, CAF148)

LPA induced CAF-associated phenotypic changes in normal ovarian fibroblasts, and glycolysis and glycolytic capacity in differentiated ovarian CAFs

LPA as a biomarker: Cao, L. et al. Diagnostic value of plasma lysophosphatidic acid levels in ovarian cancer patients: A case-control study and updated meta-analysis. J. Obstet. Gynaecol. Res. 41, 1951–1958 (2015); Li, Y. Y. et al. Plasma levels of lysophosphatidic acid in ovarian cancer versus controls: A meta-analysis. Lipids Health Dis. 14, 72 (2015).

LPA promotes migration of OC cell through different mechanisms /signaling pathway:

RhoA/ ROCK and Rac1 /PAK (Hwang, H., Kim, E. K., Park, J., Suh, P. G. & Cho, Y. K. RhoA and Rac1 play independent roles in lysophosphatidic acid-induced ovarian cancer chemotaxis. Integr. Biol. (Camb.). 6, 267–276 (2014).)

CXCL12-CXCR4 Wang, H. et al. Effect of the LPA-mediated CXCL12-CXCR4 axis in the tumor proliferation, migration and invasion of ovarian cancer cell lines. Oncol. Lett. 7, 1581–1585 (2014).

uPa/uPAR Lizalek, J. et al. Lysophosphatidic acid stimulates urokinase receptor (uPAR/CD87) in ovarian epithelial cancer cells. Anticancer Res. 35, 5263–5270 (2015).

Gai2/Src/b-pic/Rac Ward, J. D., Ha, J. H., Jayaraman, M. & Dhanasekaran, D. N. LPA-mediated migration of ovarian cancer cells involves translocalization of Gαi2 to invadopodia and association with Src and β-pix. Cancer Lett. 356, 382–391 (2015).

FOXM1 Fan, Q., Cai, Q. & Xu, Y. FOXM1 is a downstream target of LPA and YAP oncogenic signaling pathways in high grade serous ovarian cancer. Oncotarget 6, 27688–27699 (2015).

LPAR1 Yu, X., Zhang, Y. & Chen, H. LPA receptor 1 mediates LPA-induced ovarian cancer metastasis: An in vitro and in vivo study. BMC Cancer 16, 846 (2016).

NHERF1/cpERMs Oh, Y. S. et al. Dynamic relocalization of NHERF1 mediates chemotactic migration of ovarian cancer cells toward lysophosphatidic acid stimulation. Exp. Mol. Med. 49, e351 (2017).

ETS-1 Ray, U., Roy, S. S. & Chowdhury, S. R. Lysophosphatidic Acid Promotes Epithelial to Mesenchymal Transition in Ovarian Cancer Cells by Repressing SIRT1. Cell. Physiol. Biochem. 41, 795–805 (2017).

LPAR1/LPAR2/ERM Park, J. et al. LPA-induced migration of ovarian cancer cells requires activation of ERM proteins via LPA1 and LPA2. Cell. Signal. 44, 138–147 (2018).

LPAR2/ERK; LPAR3/ERK; LPAR6/AKT Onallah, H., Davidson, B. & Reich, R. Diverse Effects of Lysophosphatidic Acid Receptors on Ovarian Cancer Signaling Pathways. J. Oncol. 2019, 7547469 (2019).

LPA has also been implicated in the maintenance of CSC characteristics through AXL-LPA-LPAR1-AKT1 signaling axis in an autocrine loop. (ref: Seo, E. J. et al. Autotaxin regulates maintenance of ovarian cancer stem cells through lysophosphatidic acid-mediated autocrine mechanism. Stem Cells 34, 551–564 (2016).)

LPA treatment also leads to morphological change and alterations in the relative protein-to-lipid ratio on the OC cell surface. (ref: Bailey, K. A. et al. Chemical Analysis of Morphological Changes in Lysophosphatidic Acid-Treated Ovarian Cancer Cells. Sci. Rep. 7, 15295 (2017).)

LPA in the ascites is produced via PLA2 and ATX is secreted by tumor-associated macrophages, where the 20:4 species seems to have an important role in tumor recurrence. (ref: Reinartz, S. et al. Cell type-selective pathways and clinical associations of lysophosphatidic acid biosynthesis and signaling in the ovarian cancer microenvironment. Mol. Oncol. 13, 185–201 (2019).)

Regarding the use of LPA signaling blockade as a possible therapy, it has been found that it increases cell death in response to chemotherapeutic agents (ref: Rogers, L. A. C. et al. Blocking LPA-dependent signaling increases ovarian cancer cell death in response to chemotherapy. Redox Biol. 15, 380–386 (2018).) - see below

Blocking LPARs with an antagonist increases effect of cisplatin treatment:

Blocking LPA-dependent signaling increases ovarian cancer cell death in response to chemotherapy

Rogers et al., Redox Biology 2018; https://doi.org/10.1016/j.redox.2018.01.002

Experiment done on SKOV3 ovarian cancer cells

Cells were treated with cisplatin or paclitaxel

The increased LPA production in ovarian cancer promotes tumorigenesis, and LPA in ascites functions as a survival and proliferation signal for ovarian tumors

Blocking the LPA pathway with the LPAR antagonist VPC32183 increased the apoptosis response to cisplatin and paclitaxel

VPC32183 targets LPAR1 and LPAR3

VPC32183 treatment was done 24h prior to paclitaxel (and/or cisplatin?) treatment

VPC32183 dose was two-fold less than the dose that would induce apoptosis at 24h (?)

Concentration used was 5uM

Picture by Xu (Cancers 2018, 10, 227; doi:10.3390/cancers10070227)

Brief notes on the LPA pathway based on Lin et al. (Prostaglandins & Other Lipid Mediators, 2010; https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2845529/pdf/nihms99954.pdf) and

Xu (Cancers, 2018; https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6071084/pdf/cancers-10-00227.pdf)

LPA is produced from lysophospatidylcholine (LPC) by autotaxin (ATX), and phospholipase A2 (PLA2)

Approx. 40% of ATX is produced by adipose tissue (ex. omentum in EOC); inflammation also increases the production of ATX

LPA acts by activating 6 GPCRs: LPA1-LPA6

In ovarian cancer patients, LPA accumulation has been found in ascites and blood

LPA protects ovarian cancer cells from apoptosis, and increases metastasis and invasion mediators (urokinase plasminogen activator (uPA) and matrix metalloproteases (MMPs))

ATXi trials

What is the specific inhibition target of ATXi? the conversion from LPL to LPA? If yes, what are the side effects of targeting all the production of LPA? Why is it beneficial to combine this treatment with chemo? Why beneficial to have combinatory with PERKi and or PARPi?

ATX function Zhang X, Li M, Yin N, Zhang J. The Expression Regulation and Biological Function of Autotaxin. Cells. 2021 Apr 19;10(4):939. doi: 10.3390/cells10040939. PMID: 33921676; PMCID: PMC8073485. (review)

ATX-LPA-LPA receptor signaling axis and LPA delivery on the surface of target cell. LPA is mainly synthesized via the ATX-mediated hydrolysis of lysophosphatidylcholine (LPC) and degraded to monoacylglycerol (MAG) through LPP1/3. ATX protein is composed of two N-terminal somatomedin B (SMB)-like domains, a central phosphodiesterase (PDE) domain, and a C-terminal nuclease (NUC)-like domain. The SMB2 domain enables ATX to bind with cell surface integrin, which achieves a highly efficient delivery of LPA to its receptors. When it binds to LPAR1-6, LPA induces different downstream signal cascades, including Rho, phospholipase C (PLC), mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), phosphatidylinositide 3-kinase (PI3K), and adenylate cyclase (AC), through different G proteins.

The mechanism of ATX expression in cancer cells still not fully understood

In ovarian cancer tissue, ATX levels have been found to be at least 2x higher compared to normal ovarian tissue (ref: Umezu-Goto, M.; Tanyi, J.; Lahad, J.; Liu, S.; Yu, S.; Lapushin, R.; Hasegawa, Y.; Lu, Y.; Trost, R.; Bevers, T.; et al. Lysophosphatidic acid production and action: Validated targets in cancer? J. Cell Biochem. 2004, 92, 1115–1140.)

Furthermore, ATX activity in ascites has been found to be markedly higher than in serum or plasma (ref: as above)

The addition of an ATX inhibitor (S32826 or PF8380) abolished the production of LPA and ovarian cancer stem cells, and also reduced CSC characteristics (ref: see above on page 3, Seo E J. et al.)

“GLPG1690 is retained in the hydrophobic pocket and hydrophobic channel of ATX to prevent the formation of LPA.” (ref: above linked Zhang et al. review referring to Desroy, N.; Housseman, C.; Bock, X.; Joncour, A.; Bienvenu, N.; Cherel, L.; Labeguere, V.; Rondet, E.; Peixoto, C.; Grassot, J.-M.; et al. Discovery of 2-[[2-Ethyl-6-[4-[2-(3-hydroxyazetidin-1-yl)-2-oxoethyl]piperazin-1-yl]-8-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin -3-yl]methylamino]-4-(4-fluorophenyl)thiazole-5-carbonitrile (GLPG1690), a first-in-class autotaxin inhibitor undergoing clinical evaluation for the treatment of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. J. Med. Chem. 2017, 60, 3580–3590.)

ATX effects on cancer progression Kuppa SS, Jia W, Liu S, Nguyen H, Smyth SS, Mills GB, Dobbin KK, Hardman WJ, Murph MM. Autotaxin exacerbates tumor progression by enhancing MEK1 and overriding the function of miR-489-3p. Cancer Lett. 2018 Sep 28;432:84-92. doi: 10.1016/j.canlet.2018.05.037. Epub 2018 May 31. PMID: 29859298; PMCID: PMC6075675.

GLPG1690 selectively inhibits ATX

IC50 100nM

GLPG1690 has mainly been tested for the treatment of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), where LPAR1 is overexpressed

In IPF, collagen is excessively deposited in the lungs

However, GLP1690 has also recently been studied in breast cancer; see below for an experiment with mouse models

Inhibition of Autotaxin with GLPG1690 Increases the Efficacy of Radiotherapy and Chemotherapy in a Mouse Model of Breast Cancer

Tang et al., Molecular Cancer Therapeutics 2019; https://mct.aacrjournals.org/content/molcanther/19/1/63.full.pdf

Experiment done on a mouse model of breast cancer

ATX inhibitor GLPG1690 was combined with irradiation

GLPG1690 is currently in phase III trials for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis

The mice received 50 or 100 mg/kg of GLPG1690

Combining GLPG1690 with irradiation did not decrease tumor size significantly more compared to irradiation only

However the amount of Ki67 positive cells did decrease, among other effects

This was associated with increased levels of cleaved caspase-3 and decreased levels of Bcl-2

In combination with doxorubicin (chemo), GLPG1690 synergistically inhibited tumor growth

Safety, Pharmacokinetics, and Pharmacodynamics of the Autotaxin Inhibitor GLPG1690 in Healthy Subjects: Phase 1 Randomized Trials

van der Aar et al., The Journal of Clinical Pharmacology 2019

Clinical study on GLPG1690

Drug was administered to healthy subjects only; all participants were men

Safety, tolerability, pharmacokinetics, and pharmacodynamics of GLPG1690, a novel autotaxin inhibitor, to treat idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (FLORA): a phase 2a randomised placebo-controlled trial

Maher et al., The Lancet Respiratory Medicine 2018

https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanres/article/PIIS2213-2600(18)30181-4/fulltext

Clinical trial on idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis

Article is not open access

Dual Activity Lysophosphatidic Acid Receptor Pan-Antagonist/Autotaxin Inhibitor Reduces Breast Cancer Cell Migration In vitro and Causes Tumor Regression In vivo

Zhang et al., Cancer Research 2009; https://cancerres.aacrjournals.org/content/canres/69/13/5441.full.pdf

PERKi trials

What is the specific inhibition target of PERKi? What is the mode of action? Why beneficial to combine with ATXi and or PARPi?

Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress Signaling in Cancer Cells, Review 2020 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ajpath.2020.01.010

Molecular Pathways: The PERKs and Pitfalls of Targeting the Unfolded Protein Response in Cancer

Inhibition of endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress sensors sensitizes cancer stem-like cells to ER stress-mediated apoptosis

Fujimoto et al., Oncotarget 2016

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5239519/pdf/oncotarget-07-51854.pdf

Experiment done on the cervical cancer cell line SiHa

After 3h of culturing, sphere-forming cells were treated with the PERK inhibitor GSK 2606414 (1.5 uM), either alone or in combination with tunicamycin (0.03 uM) for 72h

When tunicamycin was combined with the PERK inhibitor, the proportion of apoptotic cells clearly increased

Analysis was done with flow cytometry

Regulation of PERK expression by FOXO3: a vulnerability of drug resistant cancer cells

Alasiri et al., Oncogene 2019

https://www.nature.com/articles/s41388-019-0890-7.pdf

The ER Stress Inducer L-Azetidine-2-Carboxylic Acid Elevates the Levels of Phospho-eIF2α and of LC3-II in a Ca2+-Dependent Manner

Roest et al., Cells 2018

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6316609/pdf/cells-07-00239.pdf
